data.title: Biggest stock movers Tuesday: ERIC, BA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures ticked higher early Tuesday as investors monitored the next moves in President Donald Trump’s evolving tariff agenda. The White House has intensified scrutiny of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports, hinting at possible tariffs targeting those sectors, while markets also braced for a busy day of corporate earnings.

Here are some of Tuesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Ericsson (NASDAQ:ERIC) +6% – Shares rose after the company reported Q1 results with GAAP EPS of SEK 1.24 and revenue of SEK 55.03B, up 3.2% Y/Y. The adjusted gross margin improved to 48.5% from 42.7%, while EBITA came in at SEK 6.9B (12.6% margin) and free cash flow before M&A stood at SEK 2.7B. Ericsson expressed confidence in its Mobile Networks business and expects the Enterprise segment to stabilize in 2025. The company approved a 2024 dividend of SEK 2.85 per share, to be paid in two installments. Looking ahead, Ericsson flagged ongoing macro and trade uncertainties, projecting Q2 adjusted gross margin in the 48–50% range, factoring in a roughly 1-point hit from tariffs, while sales across Networks and Cloud segments are expected to align with seasonal norms.

Biggest stock losers

  • Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) -11% – Shares sank after the company delivered mixed FQ3 results, with revenue missing expectations despite a 22% Y/Y increase and narrower losses. The company also announced plans to divest its Cloud Services Business, which may shift to an on-demand GPU cluster model, opening new revenue opportunities. Management reaffirmed the importance of the Ellendale campus, with its first building slated to go live by the end of 2025. Leasing updates are expected soon, and the company sees continued demand from hyperscalers along with strong growth in HPC data center capacity.
  • Boeing (NYSE:BA) -3% – Shares fell after reports that China directed its airlines to suspend deliveries of Boeing jets amid escalating U.S.-China trade tensions. The move follows President Trump's new tariffs of up to 145% on Chinese goods, prompting Beijing’s retaliatory 125% duties on U.S. imports. With China projected to drive 20% of global aircraft demand over the next two decades, the halt adds pressure to Boeing, which has already faced challenges securing major Chinese orders. The tariffs could effectively double the cost of U.S.-made aircraft, making further deliveries economically unfeasible. China is also exploring support measures for airlines leasing Boeing jets affected by the dispute.

data.title: South Korea raises chip aid package to $23B amid US tariff concerns - report
data.description:
South Korea increased its aid package for the semiconductor industry to 33T won (about $23.25B) from the 26T won package introduced last year, Reuters reported.

The move comes after calls to the government to boost support at a time of growing policy uncertainty under the new U.S. administration and intense competition from Chinese competitors, the report added, citing a statement from the Asian country's government.

Last year, it was reported that South Korea would start granting aid to chipmakers from its 26T won package from July to boost domestic semiconductor manufacturing. The package includes loans and investment capital.

South Korea will also expand the financial assistance program for the chips industry to 20T won, compared to the prior 17T won, the report noted.

The country's decision to pour more funds into its vital chip sector is aimed at helping companies cope with heavier costs as they compete worldwide.

In a meeting held on Tuesday, Finance Minister Choi Sang-mok said the government would consult with the U.S. over the Section 232 investigations into semiconductor and biopharmaceutical imports to minimize any adverse impact on domestic companies.

The Trump administration has started investigating the impact of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security, likely setting the stage for tariffs on the sectors. The probes follow President Trump exempting chips, smartphones, computers and other electronics imports from the 145% tariffs on Chinese imports. Pharma and semiconductor imports are also exempt from the baseline 10% tariff that went into effect on April 5.

Last week, South Korea unveiled emergency support measures for its auto sector, aiming to reduce the blow of U.S. tariffs on the sector, which has been increasing exports to the U.S.

The U.S., China, the EU and Japan are among countries and regions which have stepped up efforts to boost domestic chip manufacturing to stay ahead in the artificial intelligence, or AI, race. In February, it was reported that South Korea plans to secure 10,000 high-performance graphics processing units, or GPUs, this year, aiming to be competitive in the AI space.

South Korea is the base of the world's largest memory chipmakers SK hynix and Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF).

data.title: Trump hints at auto tariff reprieve as companies 'need time' to adjust supply chains
data.description:
Donald Trump has floated a potential reprieve for the 25% tariffs on automobile and auto part imports, given the industry's deeply-integrated North American supply chains, signaling yet another shift in the president's policy stance. 

"I'm looking at something to help some of the car companies with it," Trump told reporters on Monday. "They're switching to parts that were made in Canada, Mexico and other places… they need a little bit of time, because they're going to make them here."

Trump's signal was enough to lift top automakers' shares to end on a higher note on Monday: Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) +5.6%, Ford (NYSE:F) +4.1%, GM (NYSE:GM) +3.5%, Honda (NYSE:HMC) +1.9%, Toyota (NYSE:TM) +1.5%.

"We appreciate the ongoing dialogue with the administration," said Matt Blunt, president of American Automotive Policy Council, an industry group representing the Big Three automakers. "There is increasing awareness that broad tariffs on parts could undermine our shared goal of building a thriving and growing American auto industry, and that many of these supply chain transitions will take time."

Economist Art Laffer last month noted that the 25% tariffs would severely disrupt North American supply chains that have been strengthened by the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement.

He said imposing the tariff without a permanent USMCA exemption would lead to higher consumer costs, significant investment losses and inefficient production shifts, as well as erosion of industry competitiveness.

data.title: European markets look for direction amid hopes of tariff relief
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.79% to 8,198. The pound (GBP:USD) rose above $1.32, a six-month-high. UK's unemployment rate steady at 4.4% in February.

Germany (DAX:IND) +1.09% to 21,183, marking the second straight session of advances, as investors weighed fresh tariff developments. Germany’s wholesale prices rose by 1.3%Y/Y in March.

France (CAC:IND) -0.09% to 7,266. France inflation rate steady at 0.8% as expected.

Slovakia's annual inflation rate increased to 4% in March.

Industrial production in Romania declined by 3.2% Y/Y in February.

Norway's trade surplus narrowed to NOK 60.2B in March.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) edged 0.88% higher to 504.30, with auto stocks in the lead. Optimism grew over a potential pause in auto tariffs for imported vehicles and parts. This will give automakers more time to expand US-based production. The auto sector led the gains, with Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF), BMW (NEOE:BMW:CA), Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY), Stellantis (NYSE:STLA), and Renault (OTCPK:RNSDF) among the gainers. 

Coming up in the session: The economic sentiment for Euro Area and Germany expected shortly.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 2 basis points to 4.38%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 2.51%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 4.65%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK).

data.title: New tariffs? U.S. probes pharma, chip imports' impact on national security
data.description:
The Trump administration has begun investigating the impact of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security, likely setting the stage for tariffs on the sectors.

The Department of Commerce's pharma investigation covers generic and non-generic drugs, active pharma ingredients, and derivative products. The semiconductor probe covers items including leading-edge chips, chipmaking equipment, silicon wafers, and "downstream products that contain semiconductors."

The probes will look into current and projected demand for the pharma and semiconductor products, the ability of domestic production to meet demand, and potential for export restrictions. The chip probe will also assess "the ability of foreign nations to weaponize their control over semiconductors and SME supply chains."

The investigations have been initiated under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which allows the president to adjust imports if they are deemed to be a threat to national security. 

Section 232 probes must be completed within 270 days of initiation. Public comments on the investigations will be open 21 days after Wednesday.

The investigations follow the Trump administration exempting chips, smartphones, computers and other electronics imports from the 145% tariffs for Chinese imports. Pharma and semiconductor imports are also exempt from the baseline 10% tariff that went into effect on April 5.

Pharma stocks to watch: Merck (MRK), J&J (JNJ), Eli Lilly (LLY), Novartis (NVS), Novo Nordisk (NVO), AstraZeneca (AZN), 

Semiconductor stocks to watch: Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), ASML (ASML), Nvidia (NVDA), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF).

data.title: Trump admin to discard rule that caps credit card late fee at $8
France inflation rate steady at 0.8% as expected
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in France was confirmed at 0.8% in March 2025, the same as in February, remaining at its lowest level since February 2021.

Steeper declines in energy prices and manufactured goods were offset by rising prices in services, particularly insurance, as well as food, especially fresh products.  Meanwhile, tobacco prices rose at a slower pace.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices increased by 0.2%, following stagnation in the previous period, in line with initial estimates. 

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWQ)

Currency: (EUR:USD)

data.title: Barksdale Announces to raise up to C$3M in private placement
Beiersdorf Aktiengesellschaft reports Q1 results; reaffirms FY25 outlook
data.description:
• Beiersdorf Aktiengesellschaft press release (OTCPK:BDRFF): Q1 Revenue of €2.7B (+3.8% Y/Y), (+3.6% in organic terms).
  • Consumer Business Segment: Sales reach €2.3 billion (+2.3% in organic terms) against strong prior-year baseline
  • Growth for NIVEA +2.5%, Derma +11.4%, Health Care +10.8%; La Prairie –17.5%.
  • tesa Business Segment: Strong sales growth to €441 million (+10.7% in organic terms).
  • Guidance for 2025 confirmed: Organic sales growth of 4-6% expected for the Group and the Consumer Business Segment.
